chunk_summary: This section outlines a structured follow-up protocol for monitoring nutrition-safe elimination diets in food allergy management, emphasizing diagnosis review, nutrition assessment, and forward planning for reintroduction or reassessment.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of structured follow-up visits in elimination diet management.
Learn techniques for monitoring growth, nutrition adequacy, and caregiver adherence.
Identify criteria for dietitian involvement and strategies for reducing dietary restrictions.
Review protocols for reintroduction of avoided foods and reassessment of elimination trials.
key_facts: Follow-up visits prevent open-ended elimination diets and ensure structured management.
Nutrition monitoring includes weight, height, appetite, and dietary variety assessment.
Common failure patterns include reliance on low-protein plant beverages and calorie-poor school lunches.
Trigger dietitian involvement when growth falters, avoidance expands, or cost barriers arise.
Reintroduction of avoided foods should be planned with specific food forms and settings (e.g., home or supervised oral food challenge).
If symptoms do not improve during the elimination window, reassess rather than extending the trial.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: A structured follow-up visit prevents elimination diets becoming open-ended. Start by restating the current diagnosis probability and the exact avoid list, then document any reactions since the last visit, including near-miss exposures and how the family responded.
